notes: 4 clues per country, ordered hardest/most obscure -> most obvious, matching the format of the previous batches. Includes duplicate-name aliases (Turkey/Türkiye) so both keys resolve to the same facts.

clues: It's home to the longest wall ever built by humans, stretching over 13,000 miles counting all its branches.
It's the most populous country in the world for most of recorded history, only recently overtaken by India.
It gave the world paper, gunpowder, the compass, and printing.
The Great Wall and Forbidden City sit near its capital.
It's now the most populous country in the world.
It's home to the Taj Mahal, built as a mausoleum for an emperor's favorite wife.
Chess, the number zero, and the game of snakes and ladders all originated here.
It's shaped like a large peninsula jutting into the Indian Ocean.
It's made up of over 6,800 islands, though most people live on just four main ones.
It's home to the world's oldest continuously running hereditary monarchy.
It gave the world sushi, karaoke, and the bullet train.
Its flag features a red circle representing the sun.
It's one of the most densely populated countries in the world, with half its people living in and around its capital area.
It's the birthplace of K-pop and a major global exporter of pop culture.
It shares a heavily fortified border with its northern neighbor.
It occupies the southern half of the Korean Peninsula.
It's one of the most isolated and secretive countries in the world.
It's ruled by the same family dynasty that has held power since its founding in 1948.
Its capital is known for its wide, largely traffic-free boulevards and monumental architecture.
It occupies the northern half of the Korean Peninsula.
It's the least densely populated sovereign country in the world.
It was once the center of the largest contiguous land empire in history, under Genghis Khan.
Roughly a quarter of its population still lives a nomadic or semi-nomadic herding lifestyle.
It's landlocked between Russia and China.
It's the largest country in the world by area, spanning eleven time zones.
It's home to Lake Baikal, the deepest and oldest freshwater lake on Earth.
It spans two continents, Europe and Asia.
Its capital's Red Square sits beside the Kremlin.
It's the largest landlocked country in the world.
It's home to the Baikonur Cosmodrome, the world's oldest and largest operational space launch facility.
Its capital was renamed several times in recent decades, reflecting shifting political tributes.
It's the largest country in Central Asia.
It's one of only two doubly landlocked countries in the world, meaning it's landlocked by other landlocked countries.
It was a major hub of the ancient Silk Road, with cities like Samarkand and Bukhara.
It's the most populous country in Central Asia.
It borders Kazakhstan to the north.
It's home to the Darvaza gas crater, a fire pit that has burned continuously since the 1970s, nicknamed the 'Door to Hell.'
Its capital holds a Guinness World Record for having the highest concentration of white marble buildings in the world.
Much of its territory lies within the Karakum Desert.
It sits along the Caspian Sea in Central Asia.
Nearly all of its territory is covered by mountains, mostly the Tian Shan range.
It's home to Issyk-Kul, one of the largest and deepest alpine lakes in the world.
It's landlocked in Central Asia.
It borders China to the east.
It's the most mountainous country in Central Asia.
It's home to the Pamir Mountains, sometimes called the 'roof of the world.'
Its capital's name means 'Monday' in Persian, marking the day a market once opened there.
It's landlocked, bordering Afghanistan to the south.
It's often nicknamed the 'graveyard of empires' for resisting outside conquest for centuries.
It's landlocked and mountainous, dominated by the Hindu Kush range.
It sits at a historic crossroads between Central and South Asia.
It borders Pakistan to the south and east.
It's home to K2, the second-tallest mountain in the world.
It's the fifth most populous country in the world.
It was created in 1947 through the partition of British India.
It sits on the Arabian Sea in South Asia.
It's one of the most densely populated large countries in the world.
It's home to the Sundarbans, the largest mangrove forest in the world.
It was formerly known as East Pakistan before gaining independence in 1971.
It's largely surrounded by India, with a small coast on the Bay of Bengal.
It's home to Mount Everest, the tallest mountain in the world.
It's the only country in the world with a non-rectangular national flag.
It's landlocked between India and China.
The Himalayas dominate its northern border.
It measures national success using 'Gross National Happiness' instead of GDP alone.
It was the last country in the world to introduce television, in 1999.
It's landlocked in the Himalayas between India and China.
It's one of the least visited countries in the world, with tourism tightly regulated.
It's shaped like a teardrop off the southern tip of India.
It was formerly known as Ceylon, still used as a name for its famous tea.
Its administrative capital is actually a different city, Sri Jayawardenepura Kotte.
It's an island nation in the Indian Ocean.
It's formerly known as Burma.
Its capital was built as an entirely new, largely empty city in the 2000s, replacing Yangon.
It's home to thousands of ancient Buddhist temples clustered in the plains of Bagan.
It sits in Southeast Asia, bordering Thailand and India.
It's the only country in Southeast Asia never colonized by a European power.
Its capital's full ceremonial name is one of the longest place names in the world.
It's one of the world's top rice exporters.
It's shaped somewhat like an elephant's head on the map.
It's one of the world's largest exporters of coffee, second only to Brazil.
It's shaped like a long S curving along the South China Sea.
Its economy has grown rapidly since reforms in the 1980s opened it to global trade.
It borders China to the north.
It's the only landlocked country in Southeast Asia.
It's nicknamed the 'Land of a Million Elephants,' referencing an old kingdom's name.
It's among the most heavily bombed countries per capita in history, from the Vietnam War era.
It sits between Thailand and Vietnam.
It's home to Angkor Wat, the largest religious monument in the world.
Its flag is one of the only national flags to feature a building.
It endured a devastating genocide under the Khmer Rouge in the 1970s.
It sits between Thailand and Vietnam in Southeast Asia.
It's split between a peninsula and a portion of the island of Borneo.
It's home to the Petronas Towers, once the tallest buildings in the world.
It's one of the most linguistically and ethnically diverse countries in Southeast Asia.
It sits just north of Singapore and Indonesia.
It's made up of more than 17,000 islands, the most of any country.
It's the largest Muslim-majority country in the world by population.
It's home to Komodo dragons, the largest living lizards on Earth.
It stretches across Southeast Asia between the Indian and Pacific Oceans.
It's made up of over 7,000 islands.
It's one of the largest Christian-majority countries in Asia, a legacy of Spanish colonization.
It's home to some of the most text-message-heavy mobile phone use in the world.
It sits in the western Pacific, east of Vietnam.
It was known as Persia until officially adopting its current name internationally in 1935.
It's home to Persepolis, the ceremonial capital of the ancient Persian Empire.
It holds some of the world's largest natural gas reserves.
It sits between the Caspian Sea and the Persian Gulf.
It's home to the ancient region of Mesopotamia, considered the cradle of civilization.
The earliest known writing system, cuneiform, developed here.
The Tigris and Euphrates rivers both run through it.
It sits between Iran and Syria in the Middle East.
It's home to Mecca and Medina, Islam's two holiest cities.
It holds some of the largest proven oil reserves in the world.
It's the largest country in the Middle East by land area.
It occupies most of the Arabian Peninsula.
Its capital's old walled city is one of the oldest continuously inhabited urban centers in the world.
It's home to Socotra, an isolated island so unique in its plant life it's called the 'alien island.'
It sits at the southern tip of the Arabian Peninsula.
It borders Saudi Arabia and Oman.
It's home to some of the oldest frankincense trees in the world, once a hugely valuable trade good.
It's one of the oldest independent states in the Arab world.
It sits at the southeastern tip of the Arabian Peninsula.
It controls the Strait of Hormuz alongside Iran, a key oil-shipping route.
It's home to the Burj Khalifa, the tallest building in the world.
It's a federation of seven separate emirates.
It transformed from a small fishing and pearling economy into a global business hub within a few decades.
It sits on the Persian Gulf on the Arabian Peninsula.
It's the only country in the world that spans two continents through a single city, Istanbul.
It officially changed its international name to Türkiye in 2022.
It's home to Hagia Sophia, once a church, then a mosque, then a museum, and now a mosque again.
It bridges Europe and Asia at the Bosphorus Strait.
It's the only country in the world that spans two continents through a single city, Istanbul.
It officially adopted this spelling internationally in 2022.
It's home to Hagia Sophia, once a church, then a mosque, then a museum, and now a mosque again.
It bridges Europe and Asia at the Bosphorus Strait.
Its capital is considered one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world.
It's been the site of one of the most devastating civil wars of the 21st century, beginning in 2011.
It sits between Turkey, Iraq, and Lebanon.
It has a Mediterranean coastline in the Middle East.
It's home to Petra, an ancient city carved directly into rose-colored rock.
It's one of the most water-scarce countries in the world.
It hosts one of the largest populations of refugees relative to its size in the world.
It sits between Saudi Arabia, Israel, and Syria.
It's one of the only countries in the world to successfully revive an ancient language, Hebrew, as an everyday spoken tongue.
It's home to the Dead Sea, the lowest point on Earth's land surface.
Its capital is considered holy by three major world religions.
It sits on the eastern Mediterranean coast.
It's home to some of the oldest cedar trees in the world, a national symbol on its flag.
Its capital was historically nicknamed 'the Paris of the Middle East.'
It's one of the smallest countries in mainland Asia.
It sits on the eastern Mediterranean coast, north of Israel.
It's considered one of the birthplaces of winemaking, with evidence of grape cultivation over 8,000 years old.
Its capital's name comes from an old word for 'warm,' referencing local sulfur hot springs.
It sits at the crossroads of Eastern Europe and West Asia, along the Caucasus Mountains.
It borders Russia to the north and Turkey to the southwest.
It was the first country in the world to adopt Christianity as a state religion, in the early 4th century.
Its capital is considered one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world.
It's landlocked in the South Caucasus region.
It borders Georgia, Turkey, and Azerbaijan.
Its name is sometimes translated as 'Land of Fire,' tied to natural gas fires that burn from the ground.
It was one of the first places in the world to industrialize oil drilling, in the 19th century.
Its capital sits below sea level on the Caspian Sea.
It sits in the South Caucasus, between Russia, Iran, and Georgia.
